steps.prompt: Drag D. Watch ∠A and ∠C trade off.
steps.subtitle: A cyclic quadrilateral has all four corners on one circle. [∠A](magenta) and [∠C](magenta) are opposite corners — as one grows, the other shrinks. Keep an eye on their SUM.

graphic.name: ferris
graphic.caption: Four friends ride a Ferris wheel, each in a cabin on the rim. Join the cabins and you get a four-cornered shape with every corner on one circle — a cyclic quadrilateral.

steps.body:
In any cyclic quadrilateral, each pair of OPPOSITE corners is supplementary — they add to 180°.
[∠A + ∠C](magenta) = 180°, and [∠B + ∠D](orange) = 180°.
Here ∠A = 110° forces [∠C](magenta) = 70°. 'Supplementary' just means the two angles together make a straight line, 180°.

steps.prompt: Make the four corners fit on the circle
steps.subtitle: One corner is [∠C](magenta) = 110°. Drag its opposite corner [∠A](magenta) until all four corners could sit on one circle, then check.

feedback.incorrect: Opposite corners of a cyclic quadrilateral add to 180°. If ∠C is 110°, then ∠A must be 180 − 110.

steps.revealText: ∠A = 180° − 110° = 70°. Opposite corners are supplementary — they add up to 180°.

stages: [∠A](magenta) is an inscribed angle opening onto the arc from B through C to D, so that arc = 2 × ∠A.
[∠C](magenta) is an inscribed angle opening onto the other arc, from B through A to D, so that arc = 2 × ∠C.
Those two arcs together make the whole circle: 2 × ∠A + 2 × ∠C = 360°.
Divide both sides by 2: [∠A + ∠C](magenta) = 180°. Opposite corners are always supplementary.

steps.subtitle: Opposite corners of a cyclic quadrilateral are supplementary — they add to 180°.

cards.statement: Opposite angles of a cyclic quadrilateral are equal.
cards.note: No — they are supplementary, not equal. [∠A + ∠C](magenta) = 180°, so unless both are 90° they differ.

cards.statement: Opposite angles of a cyclic quadrilateral add to 180°.
cards.note: Yes — each pair of opposite corners is supplementary. [∠A + ∠C](magenta) = 180° and [∠B + ∠D](orange) = 180°.
No — opposite angles add to 180°, not 360°. So [∠C](magenta) = 180° − 70° = 110°. An angle of 290° couldn't fit inside a quadrilateral.

cards.statement: A quadrilateral is cyclic only when all four corners lie on one circle.
cards.note: Yes — every corner must sit on the circle. Only then are the opposite angles guaranteed to be supplementary.

cards.statement: In a cyclic quadrilateral with ∠A = 72°, the opposite corner ∠C = 108°.

steps.doneNote: Opposite corners are supplementary — they make 180°, never 360°, and they're equal only in the special 90°-90° case.

problems.prompt: ABCD is a cyclic quadrilateral. ∠A = 100°. Find ∠C.

feedback.incorrect: Opposite angles of a cyclic quadrilateral add to 180°.

problems.strongHint: ∠A and ∠C are opposite, so take the given ∠A away from 180°.

problems.prompt: ABCD is a cyclic quadrilateral. ∠C = 115°. Find ∠A.

feedback.incorrect: ∠A and ∠C are opposite, so they add to 180°.

problems.strongHint: ∠A and ∠C are opposite, so take the given ∠C away from 180°.

problems.prompt: ABCD is a cyclic quadrilateral. ∠A = 72°. Find ∠C.

feedback.incorrect: Opposite angles add to 180°, so ∠C is the supplement of ∠A.

problems.strongHint: ∠A and ∠C are opposite, so take the given ∠A away from 180°.

steps.subtitle: Opposite corners of a cyclic quadrilateral are supplementary. Fill in the case [∠A](magenta) = 110°.

feedback.incorrect: Opposite corners add to 180°. Take the given ∠A away from 180° to get ∠C.

steps.doneNote: ∠A + ∠C = 180°, so ∠C = 180° − 110° = 70°. Opposite corners are always supplementary.
